message:
fix(server): surface durability_state on /health + add /health/durability

Partial fix for #94 (runtime side; frankensqlite-side regressions tracked
separately in that repo).

Long-running `serve-http` instances were accumulating message_recipients
index corruption on storage.sqlite3 that the runtime's own
archive-snapshot auto-recovery kept repairing silently — meanwhile
/health returned 200 "ready" throughout, because the health probe only
reflected the last integrity-guard tick, and the guard defaulted to a
full `PRAGMA integrity_check` every 24h. External supervisors had no
way to tell "healthy" from "silently recovered twice already" without
running their own side-watchdog.

- Config default `integrity_check_interval_hours` 24→1. A full scan
  walks every page; once per hour stays well inside ambient write
  throughput on a realistic mailbox while closing the 23-hour gap
  during which real on-disk corruption stayed invisible to operators.
  Override via `INTEGRITY_CHECK_INTERVAL_HOURS` as before.
- New `probe_runtime_durability_state()` helper computes the current
  mailbox verdict (via the existing `compute_mailbox_verdict` fast-path)
  for :memory: and file-backed DBs alike.
- `/health` now includes `durability_state` in its body, flips to 503
  when the verdict is `corrupt` or `recovering` (external supervisors
  observing only HTTP status now detect the same silent-reconstruct
  state that `durability_state=corrupt` already surfaces in logs), and
  returns 200 with `status=ready_degraded` on `degraded_read_only` so
  read-heavy callers still get a success status but can opt into strict
  behavior off the new field.
- New `/health/durability` route returns the current verdict
  unconditionally at 200 with `{durability_state, allows_reads,
  allows_writes}` — dedicated endpoint for supervisors that want the
  signal without the readiness-gating side effects of /health's status
  code flip.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -100,7 +100,18 @@ pub struct Config {
     pub database_cache_budget_kb: usize,
     /// Run `PRAGMA quick_check` on pool initialization (default: true).
     pub integrity_check_on_startup: bool,
-    /// Hours between periodic full `PRAGMA integrity_check` runs (default: 24, 0 = disabled).
+    /// Hours between periodic full `PRAGMA integrity_check` runs
+    /// (default: 1, 0 = disabled).
+    ///
+    /// Historical note (#94): this used to default to 24h. In practice,
+    /// long-running `serve-http` instances can silently accumulate
+    /// `message_recipients` index inconsistencies between ticks while the
+    /// runtime's own archive-snapshot auto-recovery keeps `/health` green —
+    /// so a once-a-day cadence routinely hid real on-disk corruption for
+    /// many hours. 1h is short enough that an operator noticing stale
+    /// status flips it in under an hour, while still being long enough
+    /// that a full scan (which walks every page) stays well under
+    /// ambient write throughput.
     pub integrity_check_interval_hours: u64,
 
     // FrankenSQLite MVCC / RaptorQ
@@ -961,7 +972,7 @@ impl Default for Config {
             database_pool_timeout: None,
             database_cache_budget_kb: 512 * 1024, // 512 MiB
             integrity_check_on_startup: true,
-            integrity_check_interval_hours: 24,
+            integrity_check_interval_hours: 1,
 
             fsqlite_concurrent_mode: false,
             fsqlite_raptorq_enabled: true,
